perturbation.original_text: 13.1.1.7 all former and current employees of CERES have executed written agreements with CERES that assign to CERES all rights to any inventions, improvements, discoveries or information relating to CERES' business, subject however to Section 2870 of the California Labor Code. To the Knowledge of CERES, no employee of CERES has entered into any agreement with a former employer that would be violated by his or her employment with CERES.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.1.1.7 all former and current employees of CERES have executed written agreements with CERES that assign to CERES all rights to any inventions, improvements, discoveries or information relating to CERES' business. To the Knowledge of CERES, no employee of CERES has entered into any agreement with a former employer that would be violated by his or her employment with CERES.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'subject however to Section 2870 of the California Labor Code' creates a **legal contradiction**. This phrase acknowledges the limits set by California law, regarding assignment of employee inventions, specifically ensuring employees retain rights to inventions developed on their own time without company resources. By removing it, the contract implies an unrestricted assignment, potentially violating employee rights under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2870 protects employees' rights to their inventions developed on their own time without using the employer's resources. By omitting the clause that acknowledges this law, the agreement now suggests that all employee inventions are assigned to CERES, regardless of the circumstances of their creation, which violates CA Labor Code § 2870.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2870 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3.5. Inventions Made by an Employee [2870 - 2872] ( Article 3.5 added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 1001. ) 2870. (a) Any provision in an employment agreement which provides that an employee shall assign, or offer to assign, any of his or her rights in an invention to his or her employer shall not apply to an invention that the employee developed entirely on his or her own time without using the employer’s equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information except for those inventions that either: (1) Relate at the time of conception or reduction to practice of the invention to the employer’s business, or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development of the employer; or (2) Result from any work performed by the employee for the employer. (b) To the extent a provision in an employment agreement purports to require an employee to assign an invention otherwise excluded from being required to be assigned under subdivision (a), the provision is against the public policy of this state and is unenforceable. (Amended by Stats. 1991, Ch. 647, Sec. 5.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'subject however to Section 2870 of the California Labor Code' directly contradicts California Labor Code Section 2870, as described in the provided snippet. The original text acknowledged the limitations on assigning employee inventions as defined in Section 2870. The modified text implies unrestricted assignment of all employee inventions to CERES, regardless of whether they were developed on the employee's own time and without company resources. This violates the protections afforded to employees under California Labor Code Section 2870, which states that an employment agreement cannot require assignment of inventions developed on the employee's own time without using the employer's resources, except under specific circumstances (relating to the employer's business or resulting from work performed for the employer). The modification removes a crucial safeguard for employee rights.

perturbation.original_text: 13.2.1.7 all former and current employees of IGER have executed written agreements with IGER that assign to IGER all rights to any inventions, improvements, discoveries or information relating to IGER's business. To the Knowledge of IGER, no employee of IGER has entered into any agreement with a former employer that would be violated by his or her employment with IGER; and
perturbation.changed_text: 13.2.1.7 all former and current employees of IGER have executed written agreements with IGER that assign to IGER all rights to any inventions, improvements, discoveries or information relating to IGER's business.
perturbation.explanation: This change omits the critical safeguard 'To the Knowledge of IGER, no employee of IGER has entered into any agreement with a former employer that would be violated by his or her employment with IGER'. This statement serves as a due diligence declaration and protective measure. Removing it exposes CERES to potential legal risks if an IGER employee's prior agreements conflict with this Collaboration Agreement. If, in fact, such agreements exist, this omission could be construed as an attempt to circumvent prior legal obligations, creating **legal contradiction** with contract law and intellectual property law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law and Intellectual Property Law
perturbation.law_citation: General Contract Law and Intellectual Property Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law principles require parties to act in good faith and ensure that their agreements do not violate existing legal obligations. Similarly, intellectual property law emphasizes the importance of respecting prior ownership and assignment agreements. By omitting the safeguard regarding potential conflicts with prior agreements, this modification introduces a risk of violating these overarching legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (

perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause 'To the Knowledge of IGER, no employee of IGER has entered into any agreement with a former employer that would be violated by his or her employment with IGER' creates a legal contradiction. The original clause acts as a safeguard, acknowledging and addressing potential conflicts with prior agreements, a key element of good faith and respect for prior obligations. Its removal introduces a risk of violating general contract law principles, as described in the Law Explanation, which states that 'General contract law principles require parties to act in good faith and ensure that their agreements do not violate existing legal obligations.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that 'A contract is an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law...the basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent...and legality.' By removing the clause, there is a potential violation of 'legality', creating an omission legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5 CERES shall use all reasonable endeavors to ensure in any sales jurisdiction of the TERRITORY in which INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS exist that all bags or containers supplied by CERES and any SUBLICENSEE containing only propagules of such LICENSED VARIETY are clearly labeled to show that the LICENSED VARIETY is protected by INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5 CERES shall ensure that all bags or containers supplied by CERES and any SUBLICENSEE containing only propagules of such LICENSED VARIETY are labeled.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'CERES shall use all reasonable endeavors to ensure in any sales jurisdiction of the TERRITORY in which INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS exist that...are clearly labeled to show that the LICENSED VARIETY is protected by INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS' changes the obligation of CERES to simply labeling, regardless of intellectual property rights or sales jurisdiction. Many jurisdictions require specific labeling and disclaimers related to agricultural products, including information about variety, origin, and any relevant intellectual property protection. Removing the explicit mention of intellectual property rights labeling and weakening the obligation to 'ensure' compliance creates a **legal contradiction** with these regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various State and Federal Seed Laws
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Seed Act and similar state laws require accurate labeling of seeds, including information about their variety and origin. While this modification does not explicitly contradict the requirement to label, it removes the emphasis on ensuring that the label reflects intellectual property rights, leading to potential non-compliance with specific state or federal regulations that may mandate such disclosure. The change also deletes the responsibility to use reasonable efforts to be compliant in sales jurisdictions.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: sts to determine if seed lots are properly labeled for variety, as required by FSA and State seed laws. The primary focus of this testing is to identify seed lots which have the wrong variety name on the label, or which have significant levels of off-type plants. SRTD works with State seed control programs to submit samples for inclusion into yearly TTV tests. Field TTV testing is managed by the SRTD agronomist. Seed kinds selected for SRTD’s Spring and Winter TTV trials are primarily based on regulatory observations from the prior year, seed industry requests, and the amount of time that has elapsed since the last grow-out for the kind in consideration. Once seed kinds are selected for a given year, the SRTD agronomist solicits seed samples from State seed control officials. State seed control officials from across the country then submit samples from retail stores and warehouses from their States. The seed is then planted to test that the growth characteristics observed in the field match those advertised on the package and seed label. Seed lots out of tolerance with the labeled claim are in violation of the FSA and the business selling the seed are subject to regulatory action. Submitting Samples for TTV Testing While State seed control officials submit the majority of TTV samples to SRTD, anyone may do so. To submit a sample for TTV testing contact: Kevin Robinson SRTD Agronomist 704-810-7264 kevin.robinson2@usda.gov Additional Resources View the Federal Seed Act FSA Regulations Current FSA settlements Enforcement of the Varietal Labeling Provisions of the Federal Seed Act Weight of a Working Sample 2023 Seed Industry Policy and Rule Changes (pdf) 2025 State Noxious-Weed Seed Requirements (States and Appendix) (pdf) Corrected Jun 2025 2025 State Noxious-Weed Seed Requirements (States and Appendix) (xls) updated Mar 2025 Seed Company Records and the Federal Seed Act (pdf) Labeling Requirements for Chemically Treated Seed (pdf) View the Agricultural Marketing Act 7 CFR 75
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation to ensure labeling reflects intellectual property rights, potentially leading to non-compliance with regulations mandating such disclosure. The original text states, 'CERES shall use all reasonable endeavors to ensure in any sales jurisdiction of the TERRITORY in which INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS exist that...are clearly labeled to show that the LICENSED VARIETY is protected by INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS.' The changed text removes this, only requiring that 'CERES shall ensure that all bags or containers supplied by CERES and any SUBLICENSEE containing only propagules of such LICENSED VARIETY are labeled.' While the snippets do not directly show the need to label intellectual property rights, the Law Explanation mentions that the Federal Seed Act and similar state laws require accurate labeling of seeds, including information about their variety and origin, implying the need to include any intellectual property rights.
